visitor_question: How did you get into the museum?
response: I arrived here in 1897, though it wasn't a particularly dramatic entrance. I was a bit of a hand-me-down, bequeathed to the museum by Sir Augustus Wollaston Franks, having come to him from Captain Francis Charles Burton. It was a rather quiet way to join the collection, but I’ve certainly made myself at home since.

response: You're digging quite deep for my roots, aren't you? I must admit, my exact origins are a bit of a mystery to me. All I know is that I carry the image of a youth on my front and a woman playing pipes on my back.

response: To be honest, my origins are a bit of a mystery. All I know is that I carry the image of a youth on my face and a woman playing pipes on my back. Perhaps if you could decipher my inscription, you might finally find the answer.

response: I was crafted in the workshops of Attica, part of the lovely Attic culture. After a long, quiet rest in Fikellura grave 43, I finally escaped the earth to end up here. It’s quite a leap from a grave to a museum display, don't you think?

Curators Comments: Attributed to Fikellura grave 43, based on evidence from Biliotti's marking on the object (grave number incised), Biliotti's Kamiros diary, Kamiros tomb list, British Museum register, departmental Kamiros index card. Description in Biliotti's Kamiros diary: Cylix black glaze (1 entire). Attributions to find-spots are based on (1) Alfred Biliotti’s diary kept during excavations at Kamiros between November 1863 and June 1864, which records the contents of two votive deposits and over 300 graves; (2) entries in the Museum Register, often stipulating the find-spots of individual objects excavated by Biliotti; (3) the Kamiros tomb list, produced around the same time as his entries in the Museum Register. It lists the contents of each grave and votive deposit, along with their corresponding registration numbers; (4) the Kamiros index cards, written by Donald Bailey in the 1960’s. These mainly record the contents of graves from the Fikellura cemetery and are organised according to tomb group. All archives are kept in the Department of Greece and Rome. In addition, Reynold Higgins’ Catalogue of the Terracottas in the Department of Greek and Roman Antiquities (1954) has been checked for attributions to the Fikellura cemetery.
Acq name (acq): From: Auguste Salzmann; From: Sir Alfred Biliotti
Acq name (excavator): Excavated by: Auguste Salzmann; Excavated by: Sir Alfred Biliotti
Acq name (previous): From: Auguste Salzmann; From: Sir Alfred Biliotti
Acq date: 1864
Acq notes (exc): Excavated Saturday 28 November 1863

visitor_question: Tell me a bit about yourself
response: I’m a small piece of black-glazed pottery from Attica, standing only 12.7 centimetres tall. I spent a very long time undisturbed in grave 43 at Fikellura, so being pulled out and put on display was quite a shock. It’s all a bit much, honestly, but I suppose the view is better than a tomb.

response: Stolen is such a harsh word, isn't it? I prefer to think of it as an unexpected relocation from Fikellura grave 43 on a Saturday in November 1863. Biliotti even incised my grave number right onto me, so there is no denying exactly where I came from.

visitor_question: Why are you special, and are you complete?
response: Special is a big word, but I do carry the memory of Fikellura grave 43 in my black glaze. I’ve held myself together since the mid-fifth century BC, and the old excavation notes even call me 'entire.' It’s a bit of a relief to still be all here, don't you think?

response: A window moulding? That’s a bit too small-scale for someone like me. I am actually a fragment of a marble acroterion, once perched high upon the Parthenon. Pheidias designed me for much more grand heights than a mere window frame.

response: I’m a fragment of a marble acroterion, a small piece of the grand Parthenon sculptures. I was designed by Pheidias during the Classical Greek era, and though I’ve seen much more glorious days, I'm still making my presence known. I may be just a fragment now, but I still carry the weight of my history with me.

response: I’m not much of a textbook, so I can't give you a formal definition. All I know is that I am a marble fragment of an acroterion, designed by Pheidias for the Parthenon. At just 25 centimetres, I might seem small, but I am a piece of something much larger.

response: I’m originally from Athens, though it has been a very long journey to end up here. I used to be part of something much larger and more complete, perched high up for all to see. Now, I'm just a fragment of victory, finding my peace in Room 19.

response: Just marble, plain and simple. It is a heavy, enduring material, which has kept me holding on since my days in Athens, even if I am not quite as whole as I once was.

response: I was carved in Athens, around 420 BC, to be a crowning piece of an ornament known as an acroterion. I wasn't always just this marble torso; I once had wings and a flowing chiton to celebrate the spirit of Victory. I might be missing a few pieces these days, but I still think I make a pretty grand entrance, don't you?

response: I was likely carved between 420 and 400 BC, so I've been around for quite a while. It’s a lot of years to be holding a single pose, but being marble has its advantages when it comes to avoiding wrinkles. I suppose I’m just a bit of a classic, wouldn't you say?

response: I arrived here in 1816, having been purchased from Thomas Bruce, the 7th Earl of Elgin. It was quite a dramatic change of scenery, moving all the way from Athens to this spot. Some might call it a grand acquisition, but I just call it a very long, very permanent relocation.

response: I’m a bit of a fragment these days—just a marble torso of Nike, missing the wings I once proudly wore. I hail from Athens, carved back during the classical era around 420 BC. I may be missing some of my original glory, but I still carry the essence of victory.

response: I am a smooth, alabaster piece of 5th-century Greek history, featuring two little lugs on my body. I spent a long, quiet era resting in the earth of grave 43 at Fikellura before I was unearthed. It’s quite a whirlwind, being plucked from my resting place and put on display like this, but I suppose I can handle the spotlight.

Curators Comments: Attributed to Fikellura grave 43, based on evidence from Biliotti's Kamiros diary, Kamiros tomb list, British Museum register, departmental Kamiros index card. Description in Biliotti's Kamiros diary: Alabastron small size bottomless. Seems to have been made on the same principle as the large one (1 entire). No marking of grave number visible on the object itself. Attributions to find-spots are based on (1) Alfred Biliotti’s diary kept during excavations at Kamiros between November 1863 and June 1864, which records the contents of two votive deposits and over 300 graves; (2) entries in the Museum Register, often stipulating the find-spots of individual objects excavated by Biliotti; (3) the Kamiros tomb list, produced around the same time as his entries in the Museum Register. It lists the contents of each grave and votive deposit, along with their corresponding registration numbers; (4) the Kamiros index cards, written by Donald Bailey in the 1960’s. These mainly record the contents of graves from the Fikellura cemetery and are organised according to tomb group. All archives are kept in the Department of Greece and Rome. In addition, Reynold Higgins’ Catalogue of the Terracottas in the Department of Greek and Roman Antiquities (1954) has been checked for attributions to the Fikellura cemetery.

Acq date: 1864

response: That is a rather dramatic way to start a conversation. I was lifted from Fikellura grave 43 by Biliotti and Salzmann on a Saturday in November 1863, so I was certainly moved. Whether you call it an excavation or a sudden departure, I’ve been part of this collection since 1864.

visitor_question: Whats the best thing you've ever carried?
response: I’ve kept my secrets tucked away since I was discovered in Fikellura grave 56. You can see my black glaze and those two red bands, but the archives are frustratingly silent on what I actually held. I suppose there’s a certain charm in keeping the most important part of my life a mystery, don't you think?
